items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’m again plagued
by vague doubts...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
the envoy has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: The key creative point is that «терзают смутные сомнения» is mock-heroic/high-brow, and the parallel structure «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» is not possession but a comic “case file” linkage: each party has (i.e., has lost/is associated with) an item. The actual translation preserves the elevated phrasing well (“plagued by vague doubts”), but it mistranslates the second half as present possession (“has”), which undermines the joke logic noted in the reference (they no longer have the items; it’s about missing items / suspicion). “Envoy” is acceptable for “посол” though “ambassador” is more idiomatic; not a core problem.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Very nice, Tsar!
Very nice, hello, Tsar!

Tsar, very nice!
Hello, Tsar! Very nice!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The comedic effect depends on repetitive, banal, overly polite Soviet-style greeting formulas, made absurd by repeatedly inserting “tsar.” The actual translation keeps the repetition and awkward politeness (“Very nice… hello, Tsar…”) and thus largely preserves the gag. However, it drops the self-introduction nuance (“I’m the Tsar”) and shifts to addressing the other as “Tsar,” which slightly blurs who is being introduced and weakens the specific satire of introducing oneself with a title. Still understandable and funny-ish in context.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Matches the reference essentially verbatim and fully preserves the authoritarian, suddenly-royal performative force. No loss of humor or character voice.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the reference; preserves the archaic/high-register melancholy and the comic mismatch with the modern setting.

items.translation:
They bricked me in!
Bricked me in, demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: The line should read as the Tsar’s panicked, superstitious interpretation of being trapped (elevator), with “demons” as the agent. The translation is close and keeps the repetition. “Bricked me in” is a reasonable idiomatic equivalent of being walled up, though it loses the ellipsis/slow dawning horror of the reference. Minor tonal/fluency difference, but meaning and joke survive.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Meaning is correct and the religious framing is preserved. However, the reference’s “Behold the power…” better captures the archaic, proclamatory, quasi-biblical flourish. “That’s what … can do” is more casual/modern and slightly flattens the mock-solemnity (important given the Soviet-atheist comic edge).

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The reference aims to convey a performative MC/announcer-style invitation that feels like a public event opening up, not just an imperative. “Everybody dance!” is understandable and works on screen, but it narrows the meaning to a command and loses some of the ‘official/host’ flavor and the sense of a general invitation/open floor. The comic effect remains but is reduced.

items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: The line is a breezy criminal’s self-satisfied quip; the reference keeps the colloquial “good call” vibe. “What perfect timing!” is idiomatic and fits the situation, but it shifts the emphasis from the speaker’s pleased self-congratulation (“I dropped in well / good call on my part”) to neutral timing luck. Slight character-tone dilution, but the beat still lands.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...

and yes! Imported caviar—
eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Core creative requirement: don’t call the eggplant spread “caviar” in English, because it confuses viewers and blunts the Soviet-satire inversion (cheap spread presented as exotic delicacy). The actual translation explicitly says “Imported caviar—eggplant!”, which keeps “caviar” and thus risks misunderstanding (viewers may think it’s literally eggplant caviar as a luxury). “Imported” is fine for “заморская,” but the key negative note is violated.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
by backbreaking labor,

is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The humor depends on the escalating inventory and the telltale stumble: he says “куртка… замшевая… Три” (i.e., he initially frames it as a single jacket, then blurts “three,” revealing exaggeration/lying). The actual translation smooths this into a consistent “three suede jackets,” removing the self-incriminating hesitation and the comic beat. It also omits the performative pause/ellipsis structure that signals his improvisation. Meaning is broadly correct (he’s listing losses), but the joke mechanism is significantly weakened.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Captures the repeated structure and the deadpan resignation. Minor difference: exclamation marks push it toward emphatic/animated delivery, whereas the reference effect is more calm, melodic, and fatalistic. Still works and is fully comprehensible.

summary.interpretation: 2.91 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.58 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
